questions.stem: A subset $H$ of a group $G$ is called a subgroup when which condition holds?
options: $H$ is itself a group under the operation of $G$.
$H$ is closed under the operation only.
questions.explanation: $H$ is a subgroup of $G$ when $H$ is a group under the same operation. That requires closure, the identity, and inverses inside $H$. Closure alone is not enough, and a subgroup need not be a proper subset since $G$ itself counts.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The one-step subgroup test says a nonempty subset $H$ of a group $G$ is a subgroup if and only if which condition holds for all $a, b \in H$?
questions.explanation: The one-step test: a nonempty $H$ is a subgroup exactly when $ab^{-1} \in H$ for all $a, b \in H$. Taking $a = b$ gives the identity, taking $a = e$ gives inverses, and combining gives closure, so this single condition packages all the axioms.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The two-step subgroup test checks that a nonempty subset $H$ is closed under the operation and also closed under which other operation?
questions.explanation: The two-step test requires a nonempty $H$ to be closed under the group operation and closed under taking inverses. Together with inheriting associativity from $G$ and obtaining the identity as $a a^{-1}$, these make $H$ a subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Why must every subgroup $H$ of a group $G$ contain the identity element $e$ of $G$?
options: By assumption in the definition of subset.
The identity of $H$ coincides with the identity $e$ of $G$.
It need not: some subgroups omit $e$.
Because $H$ is closed under the operation.
questions.explanation: A subgroup has its own identity, and it must equal $e$. If $f$ is the identity of $H$ then $f * f = f$ in $G$, and cancellation in $G$ forces $f = e$. So every subgroup contains the group identity.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Every group $G$ has two subgroups that always exist, called the trivial subgroups. What are they?
options: The set of inverses and the set of squares.
questions.explanation: The two always-present subgroups are $\{e\}$, containing only the identity, and the whole group $G$. The empty set is never a subgroup because a subgroup must contain the identity, so it cannot be empty.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the set of even integers $2\mathbb{Z} = \{\dots, -2, 0, 2, 4, \dots\}$ a subgroup of $(\mathbb{Z}, +)$?
options: Only if we add $1$ to it.
questions.explanation: The even integers contain $0$, are closed under addition (even plus even is even), and contain inverses (the negative of an even number is even). By the subgroup test $2\mathbb{Z}$ is a subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$. More generally every $n\mathbb{Z}$ is a subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Is the set of odd integers a subgroup of $(\mathbb{Z}, +)$?
options: No, because it does not contain $0$ and is not closed.
Yes, it is closed under addition.
No, because the odd integers are infinite.
Yes, every odd integer has an inverse.
questions.explanation: The odd integers fail two requirements: they do not contain the identity $0$, and they are not closed, since the sum of two odd numbers is even (for instance $1 + 3 = 4$). Either failure alone disqualifies them, so they form no subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The center $Z(G)$ of a group $G$ is defined as which set?
options: $\{a \in G : ax = e \text{ for some } x\}$
$\{a \in G : ax = xa \text{ for all } x \in G\}$
questions.explanation: The center is the set of elements that commute with everything: $Z(G) = \{a \in G : ax = xa \text{ for all } x \in G\}$. It is always a subgroup of $G$, and $Z(G) = G$ precisely when $G$ is abelian.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: For any group $G$, the center $Z(G)$ is always which of the following?
options: The empty set when $G$ is non-abelian.
questions.explanation: The center is always a subgroup. It contains $e$; if $a, b$ commute with all $x$ then so do $ab$ and $a^{-1}$, so it passes the subgroup test. It equals $G$ exactly when $G$ is abelian, but even for non-abelian $G$ it is a (possibly trivial) subgroup, never empty.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A group $G$ is abelian if and only if its center satisfies which equation?
questions.explanation: Every element commutes with every other exactly when the center is the whole group: $Z(G) = G \iff G$ is abelian. A trivial center $Z(G) = \{e\}$ is the opposite extreme, occurring for groups like $S_3$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The intersection $H \cap K$ of two subgroups $H$ and $K$ of a group $G$ is always which of the following?
questions.explanation: The intersection of two (indeed any family of) subgroups is again a subgroup. Both contain $e$, so the intersection is nonempty; and if $a, b \in H \cap K$ then $ab^{-1}$ lies in each of $H$ and $K$, hence in the intersection. By contrast the union usually is not a subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Why is the union $H \cup K$ of two subgroups generally NOT a subgroup?
options: It is never a subset of $G$.
It may fail to be closed under the operation.
It may fail to contain the identity.
questions.explanation: A union of subgroups usually fails closure. In $\mathbb{Z}$, take $H = 2\mathbb{Z}$ and $K = 3\mathbb{Z}$; both $2$ and $3$ lie in $H \cup K$, but $2 + 3 = 5$ does not. The union is a subgroup only when one of the two already contains the other.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the additive group $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$, the set $\langle 4 \rangle = \{0, 4, 8\}$ generated by $4$ is a subgroup. What is its order?
questions.explanation: Starting at $4$ and adding repeatedly mod $12$ gives $4, 8, 12 \equiv 0$, so $\langle 4 \rangle = \{0, 4, 8\}$, three elements. Its order is $3$, which equals $12/\gcd(12,4) = 12/4$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the additive group $\mathbb{Z}_{6}$, which subset is a subgroup?
questions.explanation: The set $\{0, 2, 4\}$ is closed under addition mod $6$ ($2+2=4$, $2+4=6\equiv 0$, $4+4 = 8 \equiv 2$), contains $0$, and contains inverses, so it is the subgroup $\langle 2 \rangle$. The others either omit $0$ or are not closed (for instance $1+2 = 3 \notin \{0,1,2\}$).
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the center $Z(S_3)$ of the symmetric group $S_3$?
questions.explanation: $S_3$ is non-abelian, and no non-identity permutation of three symbols commutes with every other (a transposition and a 3-cycle do not commute). Hence the center is just $\{e\}$. A center equal to the whole group would force the group to be abelian.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Using the one-step test, which single computation confirms a nonempty subset $H$ is closed under inverses, by setting $a = e$?
questions.explanation: Once the test $ab^{-1} \in H$ is known to hold and $e \in H$, putting $a = e$ gives $e b^{-1} = b^{-1} \in H$ for every $b \in H$. That is exactly closure under inverses; closure under the operation then follows from a second application.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the additive group $\mathbb{Z}$, the subgroups are exactly which sets?
options: The sets of all multiples of any two integers.
questions.explanation: Every subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$ has the form $n\mathbb{Z}$ for some integer $n \ge 0$, where $n$ is the smallest positive element (and $n = 0$ gives $\{0\}$). This is proved with the division algorithm and is the additive picture behind cyclic groups.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which subset of the additive group $\mathbb{Z}$ is NOT a subgroup?
questions.explanation: The perfect squares are not closed under addition: $1$ and $4$ are squares but $1 + 4 = 5$ is not. They also lack additive inverses. The multiples of $5$, the set $\{0\}$, and $\mathbb{Z}$ itself are all subgroups.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the additive group $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$, what is the order of the subgroup $\langle 3 \rangle$ generated by $3$?
questions.explanation: Adding $3$ repeatedly mod $12$ gives $3, 6, 9, 12 \equiv 0$, so $\langle 3 \rangle = \{0, 3, 6, 9\}$, four elements. The order is $4 = 12/\gcd(12,3) = 12/3$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: For an element $a$ of a group $G$, the set $\langle a \rangle = \{a^n : n \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ is always which of the following?
options: A subset that is not closed.
questions.explanation: The set of all integer powers of $a$ is the cyclic subgroup generated by $a$, and it is the smallest subgroup containing $a$: any subgroup containing $a$ must contain all its powers. It equals $G$ only when $a$ happens to generate the whole group.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: If $H$ and $K$ are subgroups of $G$ with $|H| = 6$ and $|K| = 10$, what can be said about $|H \cap K|$ using that $H \cap K$ is a subgroup of both?
options: It divides both $6$ and $10$, so it is $1$ or $2$.
questions.explanation: Since $H \cap K$ is a subgroup of both $H$ and $K$, Lagrange's theorem forces $|H \cap K|$ to divide both $6$ and $10$. The common divisors are $1$ and $2$, so the intersection has order $1$ or $2$. It cannot be the product or anything not dividing both orders.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which statement about the relationship between a group being abelian and its subgroups is correct?
options: A non-abelian group has no abelian subgroups.
Only the trivial subgroup of an abelian group is abelian.
Subgroups of an abelian group can be non-abelian.
Every subgroup of an abelian group is abelian.
questions.explanation: Commutativity is inherited: if $G$ is abelian then any subgroup $H \subseteq G$ also has all its elements commuting, so $H$ is abelian. Note the converse fails: a non-abelian group such as $S_3$ still has abelian subgroups, like the cyclic subgroup generated by a 3-cycle.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the additive group $\mathbb{Z}_{8}$, how many elements are in the subgroup $\langle 2 \rangle$ generated by $2$?
questions.explanation: Adding $2$ repeatedly mod $8$ gives $2, 4, 6, 8 \equiv 0$, so $\langle 2 \rangle = \{0, 2, 4, 6\}$, four elements. The order is $4 = 8/\gcd(8,2) = 8/2$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Let $H$ be a nonempty FINITE subset of a group $G$ that is closed under the group operation. What extra conclusion holds?
questions.explanation: For a finite nonempty subset, closure under the operation is enough: the finite subgroup test gives that such an $H$ is a subgroup. Repeatedly multiplying any $a \in H$ by itself stays in $H$ and must cycle, producing the identity and the inverse of $a$. This shortcut fails for infinite subsets, where $\mathbb{N} \subseteq \mathbb{Z}$ is closed under addition yet is not a subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the additive group $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$, consider the cyclic subgroup $\langle 4 \rangle$ generated by $4$. List the order of $4$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$, and state whether $\langle 4 \rangle$ equals all of $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$.
questions.solution: Adding $4$ repeatedly mod $12$ gives $4, 8, 0$, so the order of $4$ is {{1}}. Since this is smaller than $12$, the subgroup $\langle 4 \rangle$ does {{2}} equal the whole group $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$.
questions.explanation: The order of $4$ is $12/\gcd(12,4) = 12/4 = 3$, giving $\langle 4 \rangle = \{0, 4, 8\}$. A generator of $\mathbb{Z}_{12}$ needs order $12$, so $4$ does not generate the group.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Let $H = 4\mathbb{Z}$ and $K = 6\mathbb{Z}$ be subgroups of $(\mathbb{Z}, +)$. Their intersection $H \cap K$ is again a subgroup, equal to $m\mathbb{Z}$ for some $m$. Find $m$, and decide whether $H \cap K$ is a subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.solution: An integer lies in both $4\mathbb{Z}$ and $6\mathbb{Z}$ exactly when it is a common multiple of $4$ and $6$, so $H \cap K$ consists of the multiples of $\text{lcm}(4,6)$, giving $m = $ {{1}}. The intersection of two subgroups is {{2}} a subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.explanation: The common multiples of $4$ and $6$ are the multiples of $\text{lcm}(4,6) = 12$, so $H \cap K = 12\mathbb{Z}$. The intersection of subgroups is always a subgroup, which is why this set is itself $12\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Consider the group $\mathbb{Z}_{6}$ under addition and its subset $S = \{0, 2, 4\}$. Determine the number of elements in $S$, and state whether $S$ is closed under addition modulo $6$.
questions.solution: The subset $S$ has {{1}} elements. Checking sums modulo $6$: $2 + 2 = 4$, $2 + 4 = 0$, and $4 + 4 = 2$, all back in $S$, so $S$ is {{2}} under addition, making it a subgroup.
questions.explanation: $S = \{0, 2, 4\}$ has three elements. Every sum of two of them reduces mod $6$ to another element of $S$, so $S$ is closed; with $0$ present and inverses inside, it is the subgroup $\langle 2 \rangle$ of $\mathbb{Z}_6$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Work in the additive group $\mathbb{Z}_{10}$. Find the order of the subgroup $\langle 2 \rangle$ generated by $2$, and the order of the subgroup $\langle 5 \rangle$ generated by $5$.
questions.solution: Adding $2$ repeatedly mod $10$ gives $2, 4, 6, 8, 0$, so $\langle 2 \rangle$ has order {{1}}. Adding $5$ gives $5, 0$, so $\langle 5 \rangle$ has order {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The order of $2$ is $10/\gcd(10,2) = 10/2 = 5$, so $\langle 2 \rangle = \{0,2,4,6,8\}$. The order of $5$ is $10/\gcd(10,5) = 10/5 = 2$, so $\langle 5 \rangle = \{0, 5\}$. Both orders divide $10$, as Lagrange requires.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra
